title: Altar Egos

jokes.text: Narrator sets up spring break in Newport Beach as George Michael spends time with his uncle — cut to Gob doing a bad card trick shirtless
jokes.setup: Narrator describes wholesome uncle-nephew bonding during spring break
jokes.punchline: The bonding activity is Gob doing a shirtless, failed magic trick on the family yacht
jokes.explanation: The narrator's framing of innocent bonding immediately undercut by Gob's shirtless incompetence is classic AD structural irony. Character-specific — only Gob would be doing this.

jokes.text: Gob misidentifies the card ('eight of diamonds') and then when corrected ('queen of diamonds') says 'No, no, no. I had... But if that had been the actual card...'
jokes.setup: Gob claims to be receiving telepathic energy to identify the card
jokes.punchline: He gets it completely wrong, and when told the right card, tries to retroactively claim near-success
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-delusion in the face of obvious failure is core character comedy. His attempt to spin a wrong answer as 'amazingly close' then pivot again is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: 'I'm still kind of tweaking my nipples... The presentation was great.' / 'Hey. Whoa!'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining he's still working out the kinks in the trick presentation
jokes.punchline: He says 'tweaking my nipples' meaning the presentation details, but Michael walks in on what is apparently a literally shirtless Gob, prompting 'Hey. Whoa!'
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed double entendre. 'Tweaking my nipples' is said innocuously about a magic trick, but Michael's 'Hey. Whoa!' reaction reveals the literal physical reality. The joke works on two levels simultaneously and lands with perfect economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'George Michael, you should get to school.' / 'No, we're off for two weeks.' — Michael doesn't know about spring break
jokes.setup: Michael acts as responsible father
jokes.punchline: He doesn't even know his son is on spring break, undercutting his authority
jokes.explanation: Mild character joke about Michael's cluelessness about his son's life despite his self-image as devoted father. Functional but not standout.

jokes.text: Spring break! It's my favorite holiday. Nothing gets me more excited. / 'I can see that. You might wanna button that shirt up.' / 'No, I'm good.'
jokes.setup: Gob declares spring break his favorite holiday
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I can see that' implies Gob is visibly aroused/shirtless; Gob dismisses it with complete unselfconsciousness
jokes.explanation: The deadpan 'No, I'm good' from Gob in response to being told to cover up is a perfect character beat. His complete comfort with his own exhibitionism is a rich running vein.

jokes.text: Flashback: Gob on a youth-oriented music network announcing he will make a yacht disappear in one year — crowd goes wild
jokes.setup: One year earlier, Gob made a grand public promise on TV
jokes.punchline: He hasn't done it, and the implication is he's been avoiding it all year — now the deadline has arrived
jokes.explanation: Classic AD structure: a year-old consequence coming due. The show pausing to contextualize Gob's request through a flashback that makes him look worse is perfectly executed. Sets up the episode's main plot while getting a laugh.

jokes.text: 'He hasn't done it, and you're holding a four of spades in your hand.' / 'I don't care. I'm loving the ride, you know?'
jokes.setup: Gob's magic is revealed to be fake on multiple levels (wrong card, unfinished yacht trick)
jokes.punchline: George Michael, aware of all of this, says he's just enjoying the experience anyway
jokes.explanation: 'I'm loving the ride' is a wonderfully layered line — George Michael is aware his uncle is a fraud but loves him anyway, and this naive devotion is itself the joke. Perfectly in character for both.

jokes.text: Michael: 'It is a path to a lonely life where people mock you. And you don't even realize it.' / 'Michael, I'm a magi...' / 'I see what you did.'
jokes.setup: Michael lectures Gob about magic being a road to mockery
jokes.punchline: Gob starts to say 'magician' and stops himself, Michael catches the self-aware pause — Gob literally can't say the word 'magician' (he insists on 'illusion'/'illusionist'), making Michael's point about mockery land doubly
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional AD layering. The joke only fully works if you know the running gag that Gob violently objects to being called a magician. His self-censoring mid-word ('I'm a magi...') and Michael's 'I see what you did' is a perfect meta-callback with tremendous economy. Character integration is as high as it gets — this joke is impossible without this specific character and this specific running gag.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Kitty, these are not the files I asked for.' / Kitty: 'Oh, then I don't know what I shredded.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Kitty about wrong files
jokes.punchline: Kitty's response implies she shredded the correct files, delivered with complete casualness as if this is not a crisis
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect economy. Six words ('I don't know what I shredded') contain a disaster, a character portrait (incompetent, unbothered), and a punchline. The casualness of her delivery is what makes it sing — it's not a crisis to Kitty, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: 'Have you had a chance to type up that report yet?' / 'No, I have been Googling your father.' / 'So I've heard.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Kitty about work; she says she's been 'Googling his father'
jokes.punchline: 'So I've heard' implies this is well-known office gossip that 'Googling' is a euphemism for something salacious between Kitty and George Sr.
jokes.explanation: Excellent double entendre. 'Googling' was new enough in 2004 to still read innocently, but Michael's deadpan 'So I've heard' instantly reframes it as office rumor about a sexual relationship. Three words do tremendous work. The timing is crisp.

jokes.text: Discussing inheritance: 'Buster got a perforated heart and Gob got that receding hairline, but you and I pretty much dodged the bullet.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks about what they got from Nana (expecting cash)
jokes.punchline: Lindsay catalogues the genetic ailments passed down — perforated heart, receding hairline — as if these are the equivalent of inheritance gifts
jokes.explanation: Dark and specific — cataloguing family genetic misfortune as an inheritance list is sharp. The specificity (perforated heart, receding hairline for Gob) makes it feel earned. Good economy.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I'll take you down to see Nana if you split the money with me 60/40.' / Michael: '55/55.' / Lindsay: 'Deal.'
jokes.setup: They negotiate a split of Nana's money
jokes.punchline: Michael offers '55/55' — which adds up to 110% — and Lindsay immediately agrees, revealing she can't do basic math
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The '55/55' is a completely absurd counter-offer that exposes Lindsay's innumeracy with devastating efficiency. Michael may or may not have meant it as a trick, but Lindsay's instant 'Deal' is the killer. It's character-perfect (Lindsay is oblivious and greedy), structurally elegant, highly quotable, and does enormous work in six words. Comparable to the '8.0 anchor' in craft.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Sounds like you guys are getting more than you think.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Michael agree on the '55/55' deal
jokes.punchline: The narrator dryly notes they're getting more than they think — more than 100% of the money — in his characteristically understated way
jokes.explanation: The narrator's wry commentary amplifies the '55/55' joke with perfect deadpan economy. The narrator-as-straight-man is a key AD device and this is a good instance of it.

jokes.text: Tobias in prison: 'I sold you for a pack of cigarettes.' / 'But didn't you come here to research the nature of fear?'
jokes.setup: Tobias came to prison to research a role; George Sr. is in prison for actual crimes
jokes.punchline: George Sr. sold Tobias (his son-in-law) to another prisoner for cigarettes — and Tobias's response reframes this as research material
jokes.explanation: Dark and character-rich. George Sr.'s casual admission of selling his son-in-law for cigarettes is delivered matter-of-factly, and Tobias's pivot to treating it as research opportunity reveals his bottomless obliviousness. Both characters in one exchange.

jokes.text: 'There's only one man I've ever called coward. That's Brian Doyle-Murray.' / 'What I'm calling you... is a television actor.' / 'Ouch.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. builds up to calling Tobias a coward with theatrical gravitas, referencing Brian Doyle-Murray
jokes.punchline: The devastating insult is not 'coward' but 'television actor' — delivered as if this is worse, and Tobias (who desperately wants to be a film actor) agrees it is
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The misdirection is exquisite: George Sr. builds enormous expectation ('There's only one man I've ever called coward...') with Brian Doyle-Murray as an absurd specific reference, then delivers 'television actor' as the ultimate insult. Tobias's 'Ouch' confirms the severity. It only works because Tobias's failed acting career and desperate desire for prestige are established character facts. The Brian Doyle-Murray name-drop adds a layer of absurdist specificity. Near-perfect structure, character integration, and earned humor. Comparable to the 9.5 anchor in structure and character integration.

jokes.text: Lindsay and Maeby arrive at 'Nana's' — it's not Nana. 'A fact that took Lindsay and Maeby an hour and a half to ascertain.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Maeby arrive at Nana's address to get birthday money
jokes.punchline: The woman there isn't Nana — and they spent an hour and a half before figuring that out
jokes.explanation: The 'hour and a half' detail is the killer — the narrator's deadpan precision implies they had a full, involved interaction with a stranger before realizing. This is AD at its best: what happened in that hour and a half is funnier for being unseen.

jokes.text: 'Go! Go! It's not her! Drop the photo album!' / 'We're not in the photo album!' / 'Drop the pie!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay and Maeby realize they've been visiting the wrong woman
jokes.punchline: Their panicked exit reveals they had already gotten deeply embedded — they're in the photo album, they took a pie — the escalation of what they took/did keeps adding details
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire revelations ('We're not in the photo album!' 'Drop the pie!') are brilliant. Each new detail escalates the absurdity of how embedded they got with a stranger. 'We're not in the photo album' is particularly rich — it means they checked, and were disappointed not to be. Excellent escalation structure.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'Much better. Man, I was really stuffed up there!' — gesturing below her chin, Michael looks up
jokes.setup: Michael asks how Kitty is feeling after her illness; she says she was stuffed up
jokes.punchline: 'Up here, Michael. Up here.' — Kitty has visibly had breast implants during her sick leave, and Michael was staring
jokes.explanation: 'Up here, Michael. Up here' is the episode's most quotable running line. The reversal of the classic 'eyes up here' rebuke — she WANTS him to look at her new implants — is a strong character-driven subversion. The 'stuffed up' double meaning is nice wordplay.

jokes.text: Narrator explains Kitty wasn't included in a Girls With Low Self-Esteem video the previous spring break — shown producer saying 'We got all the good stuff here'
jokes.setup: Narrator explains why Kitty is so eager for this spring break
jokes.punchline: She was rejected from a 'Girls With Low Self-Esteem' video — the title itself is the joke (the series exploits women with low self-esteem, and Kitty wasn't even good enough for that)
jokes.explanation: AD's 'Girls With Low Self-Esteem' parody of 'Girls Gone Wild' is sharp cultural commentary. Kitty being rejected from even this is darkly funny and establishes her motivation with precision.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'Break? You were just out.' / 'That was my sick leave. This is vacation.'
jokes.setup: Michael objects to Kitty taking more time off right after returning from sick leave
jokes.punchline: Kitty draws an icy distinction between sick leave and vacation as if these are obviously separate entitlements — no acknowledgment of how absurd this is
jokes.explanation: Kitty's deadpan entitlement is well-executed. The legal hair-splitting ('sick leave' vs 'vacation') from an employee who just shredded the wrong files is a nice contrast.

jokes.text: Kitty describes going to Senor Tadpoles 'having a margarita made in my mouth' — Michael: 'There will be no margarita in your mouth!' / Kitty: 'Oh, yes, there absolutely will be a margarita in my mouth!'
jokes.setup: Kitty describes her spring break plans with increasing defiance
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'margarita made in my mouth' is inherently absurd/sexual, and Michael's earnest denial/her emphatic counter-denial turns it into an absurd battle of wills
jokes.explanation: The escalating margarita argument is funny through sheer specificity and Kitty's absolute certainty. The phrase 'margarita made in my mouth' is inherently comic — it sounds sexual while being literally a spring break activity.

jokes.text: Kitty flashes Michael during the argument: 'Put the shirt back down and keep on moving.'
jokes.setup: Kitty is being fired; she escalates by flashing
jokes.punchline: Michael's response ('Put the shirt back down') is utterly world-weary, as if this is just a Tuesday
jokes.explanation: Michael's bone-tired response to being flashed is the joke — his complete lack of surprise or titillation. The 'keep on moving' reduces it to traffic management.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'I work for your father. You don't have the hiring and firing power.' — after being fired
jokes.setup: Michael tries to fire Kitty
jokes.punchline: Kitty correctly points out he lacks authority — which is humiliating for Michael but also reveals the absurdity of the family company's power structure
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the recurring 'Michael can't fire Kitty' thread. Not the sharpest version of this joke but it establishes the power dynamic.

jokes.text: Kitty flashes the restaurant: 'But take a good look, 'cause it's the last time!'
jokes.setup: Kitty has been flashing throughout the scene
jokes.punchline: 'Take a good look, 'cause it's the last time' — a callback to Gob's magic show patter, now applied to Kitty flashing a restaurant
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully planted callback — Gob's magician patter ('take a good look, 'cause it's the last time') is repurposed by Kitty flashing a restaurant. It connects the episode's two main plot threads through language. Highly quotable and rewards rewatching.

jokes.text: George Michael, at work: 'That's the seventh nipple I've seen today.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has had an absurd spring break — Gob shirtless, Kitty flashing
jokes.punchline: 'Seventh' implies a precise running tally maintained by a teenage boy who was hoping for a different kind of spring break — the number is specific and devastating
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top three jokes. The specificity of 'seventh' is the genius — it implies George Michael has been keeping count, which reveals both the absurdity of his day and his teenage preoccupation with nipples. The deadpan delivery in what should be a cheerful spring break context is perfect character work. It's a running-gag callback that also stands alone as a great line. Extremely high economy — four words do everything.

jokes.text: George Sr. on Kitty's chest: 'Oh, my God, you paid for those, didn't you?' — implying he paid for Kitty's implants
jokes.setup: Michael tells his father about Kitty's new appearance
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s first instinct is not concern about Kitty but whether the company (he) paid for the implants — revealing the full corrupt nature of their relationship
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the implication — George Sr. immediately recognizing his own financial culpability reveals the full scope of his relationship with Kitty without needing to state it explicitly.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I even enjoyed firing my own twin brother. You should have seen his face when he was begging me not to. Well, he's my twin brother. I can show you.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is discussing his corruption and love of power
jokes.punchline: He offers to demonstrate his twin brother's begging face — because he can, being his identical twin — which is both deeply wrong and absurdly funny
jokes.explanation: Brilliant and dark. The logic of 'he's my twin, so I can show you his face' is uniquely absurd — George Sr. plans to imitate his own twin brother begging. The dark joy he takes in this is perfect character work. Only this specific character in this specific situation could make this joke.

jokes.text: Reaction beat: George Sr. imitates his twin brother's begging face for Michael
jokes.setup: George Sr. offers to show his twin's face
jokes.punchline: He presumably does an impression of himself begging — the audience sees this; Michael's visible discomfort/disgust is the payoff
jokes.explanation: The visual payoff of George Sr. actually doing the impression elevates the joke. Michael's reaction ('It was funny') is itself a bit of dark comedy — he found it funny, which is a small character moment.

jokes.text: 'Oh, God, there's nothing to hold on to down there. You're like a boy.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Michael are apparently physically close in the prison visiting area
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s comment about Michael having nothing 'down there' to hold onto is alarming in context — it's deeply inappropriate and weirdly emasculating
jokes.explanation: The creepiness of George Sr. commenting on Michael's physicality in this way is the joke — it's so inappropriate that it transcends normal embarrassment. The 'you're like a boy' kicker is the landing.

jokes.text: 'All these books are cooked!' / 'I'm sorry. I gotta do my job.' / 'And I gotta get out of prison.'
jokes.setup: Michael discovers George Sr.'s financial crimes while visiting
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s 'I gotta get out of prison' is delivered as a perfectly equivalent moral counterpoint to Michael's 'I gotta do my job' — as if fraud and compliance are equal professional obligations
jokes.explanation: The parallel structure ('I gotta do my job' / 'I gotta get out of prison') draws a dark comic equivalence between Michael's ethics and George Sr.'s needs. George Sr.'s total lack of shame is the consistent joke.

jokes.text: 'Next time someone threatens me, I may not have a son-in-law to offer.' — corrects to 'offer me counseling'
jokes.setup: George Sr. seems about to reveal he offered Tobias to White Power Bill as a sex trade
jokes.punchline: He corrects himself to 'offer me counseling' — but the slip revealed the truth, and Michael's alarm ('"Offer"?') confirms the audience heard what they heard
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection. George Sr.'s Freudian slip ('I may not have a son-in-law to offer') and his fumbled correction is a masterclass in implication. Michael's one-word response ('Offer?') does everything — confirms, accuses, reacts — in one syllable. The dark backstory is laid out through what's almost said.

jokes.text: Narrator: Tobias 'produced a videotape... but its brief success was due to its misleading name. Once discovered, all but 10 of the videos were returned.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. says Tobias is gifted as a shrink; narrator contextualizes with a videotape Tobias made
jokes.punchline: The tape's 'misleading name' caused it to be briefly successful — strongly implied to be a title that sounds like pornography but is actually therapeutic. Only 10 of an unknown number were kept.
jokes.explanation: Classic AD technique: the narrator implies something outrageous without stating it explicitly, the joke existing entirely in what's left out. 'Misleading name,' 'brief success,' and 'all but 10 returned' construct a complete and hilarious story about Tobias accidentally making therapy videos that were mistaken for pornography. The precision of 'all but 10' is the perfect Tobias-specific detail.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'There's just absolutely no purse that will go with this outfit.' — while in a prison visiting room
jokes.setup: Lucille is visiting her husband in prison
jokes.punchline: Her dominant concern is handbag coordination with her prison-visit outfit — the absolute mismatch of priorities (fashion vs. husband in prison)
jokes.explanation: Lucille's vanity in the face of a prison visit is perfectly character-specific. No other character could or would say this. The setup (visiting imprisoned husband) and punchline (handbag coordination) are perfectly mismatched priorities.

jokes.text: 'You've got a little pocket there, Annyong.' / 'Annyong.' / 'That's not getting old.'
jokes.setup: Annyong, the adopted Korean boy, responds to everything with 'Annyong' (which means 'hello' in Korean, but the family thinks it's his name)
jokes.punchline: Buster/someone remarks 'that's not getting old' — sarcastic acknowledgment of the running gag
jokes.explanation: The meta-acknowledgment 'that's not getting old' is the show winking at its own running gag. It IS getting old (that's the point), but the self-awareness makes it work.

jokes.text: Lucille describes her dead mother as if she's alive and on a cruise: 'She just came back from a wonderful costume party the captain threw. She gained 10 pounds — there's so much food on that boat. She's up to 74!'
jokes.setup: Lucille claims her mother is alive and on a cruise
jokes.punchline: The details are absurdly specific and cheerful — Lucille is maintaining an elaborate fiction about her dead mother to control the inheritance. 'She's up to 74!' as a weight milestone is particularly dark.
jokes.explanation: Lucille's cheerful fabrication about her dead mother is dark and perfectly in character — her love of control and money overrides any grief. The specificity of 'up to 74 pounds' on a dead woman is wonderfully macabre.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'In fact, Lucille's mother had been dead for six months.'
jokes.setup: Lucille has just delivered an elaborate story about her mother on a cruise
jokes.punchline: The narrator's flat statement: dead for six months. Complete contrast with Lucille's warmth.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's timing is everything — he waits until the end of Lucille's elaborate story and then delivers the truth in one sentence. Classic AD narrator structure at its most effective.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'She tried pesto for the first time. Can you believe that? Ninety-two years old and she never tried pesto.'
jokes.setup: After narrator reveals Nana has been dead six months, Lucille continues the lie
jokes.punchline: Lucille pivots from describing a costume party to mourning that her dead mother never had pesto — a trivially mundane detail to fixate on about someone who's been dead six months
jokes.explanation: After we know Nana is dead, Lucille's continued fiction about pesto becomes a layer darker. The mundane specificity of pesto as the detail she fixates on is brilliantly absurd. 'Ninety-two years old and she never tried pesto' would be a mild anecdote — knowing the woman is dead for six months makes it extraordinary.

jokes.text: Annyong emery board: Lucille uses Annyong as a living accessory, handing him an emery board. 'Isn't he great? And he goes with everything.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is filing her nails; Annyong is nearby
jokes.punchline: 'He goes with everything' — Lucille treats her adopted child like a fashion accessory, praising him the way she would a handbag
jokes.explanation: Perfectly distilled Lucille character comedy. 'He goes with everything' applies Lucille's fashion-accessory worldview to her adopted child. The earlier callback about the purse ('There's just no purse that will go with this outfit') makes 'goes with everything' a direct substitution. This is exactly the kind of thing only Lucille would say.

jokes.text: Gob instructing a woman: 'If you'll be so kind as to expose your breast, please.' — George Michael walks in
jokes.setup: Gob is supposedly doing a magic trick involving a T-shirt and a hat
jokes.punchline: His instruction to 'expose your breast' is delivered in the mannered, formal language of a magician, giving the request a thin veneer of legitimacy that fools no one — especially not George Michael who has walked in
jokes.explanation: Gob's insistence on formal magician's phrasing ('if you'll be so kind') while requesting something he has no professional right to ask is excellent character work. George Michael's mortified intrusion elevates it.

jokes.text: Michael: 'What the hell are you doing?' / Gob: 'Dad, it's misdirection. You're supposed to keep your eye on the shirt in the hat.'
jokes.setup: Michael catches Gob asking a woman to expose herself
jokes.punchline: Gob's genuine defense is that it's 'misdirection' — a real magic technique — applied here to distract Michael from the actual predatory behavior
jokes.explanation: Gob using a real magic term to legitimize something illegitimate is a layered meta-joke about how he weaponizes his profession. The specific technical language ('misdirection') delivered with complete sincerity is the key.

jokes.text: 'Buddy, what are you doing here?' / 'I'm dying to see how he makes this yacht disappear.' / 'He's not going to make the yacht disappear. I told him that.' / 'It's driving me crazy. It's like a mind puzzle, an awesome mind puzzle.'
jokes.setup: Michael is exasperated about Gob's magic show
jokes.punchline: Buster's childlike wonder ('an awesome mind puzzle') about a magic trick that Michael has correctly identified as impossible reveals Buster's complete intellectual innocence
jokes.explanation: Buster's characterization as an overgrown child is perfectly deployed here. 'An awesome mind puzzle' from a man in his 30s is exactly the right note for the character.

jokes.text: 'Don't worry. He's gonna be covered with a blue dot. He approached me.' — Gob about George Michael being in a Girls With Low Self-Esteem video
jokes.setup: Michael discovers his teenage son was nearly in a Girls With Low Self-Esteem video
jokes.punchline: Gob reassures him that George Michael will be 'covered with a blue dot' — the standard TV censorship for such content — as if this is entirely adequate reassurance
jokes.explanation: The 'blue dot' detail is specific, dark, and reveals Gob's complete moral blindness — he thinks censorship technology resolves the ethical problem of filming someone's underage kid. Only Gob would offer this as comfort to a father.

jokes.text: Gob post-intimacy becomes 'really girlie' — 'He's my brother, and he's never even said 'Good job.' I just want him to love me, you know?' Michael: 'Wow, you get really girlie after, huh?'
jokes.setup: Gob is supposed to be discussing the Kitty situation and the yacht deal
jokes.punchline: Gob dissolves into emotional vulnerability about wanting Michael's approval — a complete tonal whiplash from his usual bravado — and Michael's response is 'you get really girlie after, huh?' acknowledging the post-coital emotional register
jokes.explanation: Gob's emotional neediness post-intimacy is a great character beat — he's ALL bravado except for these unexpected moments of desperate sibling longing. Michael's deflating 'you get really girlie after' is perfectly calibrated. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Gob after sex: 'Wow, you get really girlie after, huh?' — Michael ignores this
jokes.setup: Gob has just finished an emotional moment about wanting Michael's approval
jokes.punchline: Michael (or another character) calls out Gob's sudden emotional vulnerability as 'really girlie' — Gob's typical toxic-masculine posturing collapses post-coitally into neediness
jokes.explanation: Gob's post-emotional vulnerability and the immediate mockery of it is character-specific. The joke is that Gob, who projects hypermasculinity, has just expressed genuine need for his brother's approval.

jokes.text: Tobias doing therapy on White Power Bill: 'Yes, but where does the hate come from, Bill? What causes it?' / 'The Jews, I guess.' / 'Sure. But I think you need to look deeper.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is doing amateur therapy on a neo-Nazi prisoner
jokes.punchline: White Power Bill gives an antisemitic answer; Tobias accepts it with 'Sure' and redirects to deeper psychology — treating the answer as a valid if shallow starting point for therapy
jokes.explanation: Tobias's therapeutic 'Sure. But I think you need to look deeper' in response to 'The Jews, I guess' is brilliantly dark. His non-condemnation of the answer (just treating it as surface-level thinking) is both hilarious and extremely specific to his oblivious character. The therapy-speak applied to neo-Nazi psychology is uncomfortable and funny.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I know what it's like to have a father, or, in my case, a father-in-law, who doesn't respect you.' Then: 'Anyhoo, we have very little time before this, uh, 4:00 pounding you promised me.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is having a breakthrough therapy session with his white supremacist cellmate
jokes.punchline: 'The 4:00 pounding you promised me' — Tobias cheerfully references a scheduled prison beating with the same equanimity he brings to everything, treating it as a calendar appointment
jokes.explanation: 'Anyhoo' followed by cheerful scheduling of his own beating is quintessential Tobias — completely unaware of the severity of his situation, bringing his relentlessly positive theatrical affect to prison violence. High character integration; only Tobias could deliver this.

jokes.text: Tobias's breakthrough therapy: 'I think you hate White Power Bill!' / 'I hate you!' / 'You hate White Power Bill!' / 'I hate the government!' / 'You hate White Power Bill!' / 'I hate my father!'
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to redirect White Power Bill's self-hatred
jokes.punchline: Each of White Power Bill's redirections ('I hate you!' 'I hate the government!' 'I hate my father!') is met with Tobias's same insistent 'You hate White Power Bill!' — a therapeutic technique that becomes increasingly farcical
jokes.explanation: The repetition structure ('You hate White Power Bill!') is precisely calibrated — each cycle escalates White Power Bill's deflection while Tobias's insistence becomes more absurd. The fact that Tobias is RIGHT (this is how therapy actually works) while being hilariously oblivious to the danger makes it doubly funny. Strong escalation with perfect comic rhythm.

jokes.text: 'I hate White Power Bill.' — White Power Bill attacks someone / 'I got a prisoner down! Prisoner down!' / 'You killed him.' / 'Like Dorothy. The Wicked Witch is dead.' / 'All hail Dorothy!'
jokes.setup: Tobias's therapy succeeds — White Power Bill attacks his own self-projected hate
jokes.punchline: White Power Bill apparently kills someone/himself; Tobias's Wizard of Oz reference ('Like Dorothy') and the prisoners chanting 'All hail Dorothy!' is absurdist escalation of the darkest kind
jokes.explanation: The Wizard of Oz callback ('Like Dorothy. The Wicked Witch is dead. All hail Dorothy!') applied to a prison violence incident is an inspired left turn. The prisoners spontaneously chanting in unison is absurdist excellence. Tobias has accidentally caused a prison death via therapy — and references a children's film. Dark and original.

jokes.text: Kitty to Michael at restaurant: 'Who are you asking back, Michael? Me? Or these?'
jokes.setup: Michael asks Kitty to come back to work
jokes.punchline: Kitty personalizes the request to her new implants as a separate entity requiring their own invitation
jokes.explanation: Kitty treating her implants as distinct entities with their own social needs is perfectly absurd and character-specific. The line 'me? Or these?' efficiently packages her vanity and the implants' prominence in one breath.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'I never really left, because we both know that you can't fire people.' / Michael: 'No, I did fire you, and now I'm hiring you back.' / 'You don't have authority over me.' / 'They don't have nachos here.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to rehire Kitty with dignity
jokes.punchline: The negotiation collapses with Kitty disputing Michael's authority; Michael's non-sequitur 'they don't have nachos here' suggests he's so rattled he's reading the menu
jokes.explanation: The nachos non-sequitur reveals Michael's total loss of composure — he's looking at the menu to escape the conversation. Small but effective character beat.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'Did you hear that, everyone? Michael Bluth is threatening me!' — then: 'I've gotta get out of here. I'm a part of the story. I can't be a part of the story.'
jokes.setup: Kitty and Michael's dispute escalates publicly in a restaurant
jokes.punchline: Kitty makes a public scene and then seemingly refers to 'the story' — possibly the news story, possibly breaking the fourth wall — while fleeing
jokes.explanation: Kitty's theatrical public announcement and self-aware exit ('I'm part of the story') reads as both realistic (she's calling witnesses) and potentially meta-textual. The rapid character shift from threatening to fleeing is good comic escalation.

jokes.text: Kitty: 'And say good-bye to these.' / Then flashes the restaurant / 'Cause it's the last time.'
jokes.setup: Kitty's final escalation in the restaurant scene
jokes.punchline: 'Cause it's the last time' — the callback to Gob's magic patter, now used for the third (and most public) time
jokes.explanation: The third deployment of 'it's the last time' in the context of public nudity is now a fully established running gag. Its callback to Gob's magic show language, now applied to Kitty flashing a steakhouse full of people, is peak AD layering. The public venue escalation makes it the most outrageous version yet.

jokes.text: News chyron/narrator: 'A woman shows all during a fracas at a local restaurant, sources say.'
jokes.setup: Kitty has just flashed an entire restaurant
jokes.punchline: The incident is immediately and matter-of-factly reported as news — the formality of 'sources say' applied to someone flashing a steakhouse
jokes.explanation: The deadpan news-brief format applied to Kitty's flashing is a classic AD technique — treating absurd events with journalistic gravity. 'Sources say' is the perfect flourish.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Isn't this much more fun, working with your Dad?' — George Michael's dead-eyed non-response
jokes.setup: Michael has pulled George Michael away from Gob's magic show and put him to work in the office
jokes.punchline: Michael asks if this is more fun — it obviously isn't — and George Michael's non-answer is the joke
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness to his son's misery is a reliable character note. The reaction beat from George Michael carries the comedy.

jokes.text: George Michael answers phone: 'Good afternoon. Bluth Company.' / 'Talk you off? Talk you off what, Pop-Pop?'
jokes.setup: George Michael answers the office phone professionally
jokes.punchline: 'Talk you off' — a phone sex/masturbation euphemism that George Sr. has apparently used with Kitty, and now accidentally deployed on his innocent teenage grandson
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The double entendre requires no explanation but is perfectly constructed: George Sr. calls expecting Kitty (for phone sex), George Michael answers, and his innocent 'Talk you off what, Pop-Pop?' exposes the nature of the call while being completely wholesome from his perspective. The 'Pop-Pop' formality makes it more cringeworthy. Perfect character integration — only works with these two characters in this exact situation. Extremely high economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'What did he say?' / George Michael: 'If I clean it up, it's not really a sentence.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has said something profane/obscene to George Michael on the phone
jokes.punchline: George Michael's measured 'if I clean it up, it's not really a sentence' implies what George Sr. said was so profane it loses all meaning when sanitized
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest lines. 'If I clean it up, it's not really a sentence' is a masterclass in economy — it tells us everything about what George Sr. said without saying any of it, and it characterizes George Michael's precise, careful speech perfectly. The line is infinitely quotable and rewards the viewer's imagination. Stands alongside the 8.0 anchor easily.

jokes.text: George Sr. calls someone: 'Kitty's on the loose. I need you to make something disappear for me... just some books in that cooler marked Maddas.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is worried about evidence Kitty might expose
jokes.punchline: 'Maddas' is 'Saddam' spelled backward — George Sr.'s cooler of incriminating books is labeled with a reversed Saddam Hussein reference, implying illegal arms or oil dealings
jokes.explanation: The 'Maddas' detail is an exceptional rewatch joke — on first viewing, listeners may miss that it's 'Saddam' backward, and the full implication (George Sr. has been doing business with Saddam Hussein's regime) is planted as a seed for later seasons. Pure AD foreshadowing as comedy.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'You painted your shoes red.' / 'Call me the Tin Man. I'm a Friend of Dorothy's now. And she wants to see you.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has apparently befriended the prison gang leader ('Dorothy') through the Wizard of Oz subplot
jokes.punchline: 'Friend of Dorothy' is a classic gay euphemism — Tobias is using it in its Wizard of Oz literal sense, but it has a double meaning that neither he nor George Sr. appears to recognize
jokes.explanation: Another masterclass in AD layering. 'Friend of Dorothy' is both literally true (he's friends with the prison gang leader named Dorothy) and a well-known gay euphemism — which fits Tobias's characterization as a man who is obliviously coded as gay throughout the series. The painted red shoes as the Tin Man reference is also absurd character-specific comedy (Tobias as an actor throwing himself into a role). Only Tobias could make this joke exist.

jokes.text: Michael asks about Nana; Lindsay: 'I think Mom sent her to a nursing home, some gray, windowless room... against her will.' — cut to Lucille on phone
jokes.setup: Lindsay suspects Lucille has done something terrible with Nana
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's suspicion (nursing home) is actually worse than the truth AND better — Nana is dead, not in a nursing home, but Lucille has been lying about that too
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony (we know Nana is dead; Lindsay assumes nursing home) does the work here. Lindsay's concern being misplaced in both directions is darkly funny on rewatch.

jokes.text: Michael asks George Michael how the weather is — George Michael answers 'Nice. Sunny.' — then Maeby plants the idea to sneak on the yacht without Michael noticing
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to get George Michael enthusiastic about office work
jokes.punchline: George Michael's dead 'Nice. Sunny.' shows total disengagement; Maeby is simultaneously playing Michael to get George Michael sent to the yacht
jokes.explanation: George Michael's minimal engagement with his father's enthusiasm is a good character beat. Maeby's manipulation of the scene is a nice structural moment but the comedy is subtle.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'The only real way to find out how it's done is to sneak on the boat while he does it.' / George Michael: 'But if he makes it disappear, won't everyone see me standing there?' / 'Let's just sit quietly and consider how ridiculous that statement was.'
jokes.setup: Maeby suggests George Michael sneak on the yacht to see the trick
jokes.punchline: George Michael's question ('won't everyone see me standing there?') is so perfectly literal and obtuse that Maeby can only suggest they both sit with how dumb it was
jokes.explanation: Exceptional. George Michael's question is simultaneously the logical conclusion of his literal thinking AND completely idiotic (if the yacht disappears, he disappears with it — the problem isn't being seen, it's being sunk). Maeby's 'let's just sit quietly and consider how ridiculous that statement was' is a perfect comic response that's also character-perfect for her world-weariness toward her cousin. Both characters crystallized in one exchange.

jokes.text: Gob suddenly remembers his promise to Michael and sends George Michael away: 'None of your damn business. Get the hell out of here!' / 'You don't have the magic in you! You never did!'
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives to help Gob with the magic show; Gob suddenly remembers he promised Michael to stay away
jokes.punchline: Gob deflects by attacking George Michael's magical potential — a devastating critique delivered to spare his feelings (or Michael's feelings) but which lands as pure cruelty
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the mechanism — Gob covers for his promise by attacking George Michael's magic ability, which is both cruel and utterly transparent. 'You don't have the magic in you!' is absurd as a serious criticism and devastating as a thing to say to an eager teenager.

jokes.text: 'So take a look. It's the last time you're gonna see these.' / George Michael: 'Sorry, kid, more than you'll ever...' / 'What?' / 'Beat it!'
jokes.setup: Gob is sending George Michael away and using the 'last time' phrasing
jokes.punchline: Gob appears to be about to flash George Michael ('take a look, it's the last time you're gonna see these') — applying Kitty's phrasing to himself — before catching himself
jokes.explanation: The 'it's the last time' phrase has now fully infected both Gob and Kitty's vocabulary. Gob's instinct to show George Michael 'these' (his chest? his magic?) and then catching himself is a perfectly dark near-miss. The running gag is now so established it can create a joke out of almost-deployment.

jokes.text: George Sr. visits Tobias in prison — the man 'he once tried to shove out of a moving car'
jokes.setup: Narrator introduces George Sr.'s prison visit to Tobias
jokes.punchline: The narrator's casual aside — 'someone he had once tried to shove out of a moving car' — is delivered as if this is unremarkable background information
jokes.explanation: The narrator treating attempted murder as an incidental biographical fact is classic AD tone. The specificity of 'moving car' and the casual subordinate clause structure make it excellent. 'Once tried to shove out of a moving car' as a relationship descriptor is darkly perfect.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I thought the two of us could talk, man-on-man.' / Tobias: 'I'm afraid I'm serious.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. proposes a serious conversation with Tobias
jokes.punchline: 'Man-on-man' as an alternative to 'man-to-man' is a classic Tobias inadvertent double entendre — and Tobias's 'I'm afraid I'm serious' suggests he doesn't catch his own slip
jokes.explanation: Tobias's inadvertent gay double entendres are a series-defining running gag, and 'man-on-man' is a particularly clean example. The fact that he then says 'I'm afraid I'm serious' — not clarifying the slip, but affirming the conversation's gravity — is perfect. Character integration is essentially perfect: this joke cannot exist without Tobias.

jokes.text: George Sr. gives Tobias tea in prison: 'Remember to let it steep for five minutes.' / 'Oh, dear God.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is trying to have a meaningful conversation with Tobias
jokes.punchline: Tobias's response 'Oh, dear God' to steeping instructions — implying George Sr.'s domestic normalcy in a prison context is itself unbearable
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the mundane domestic detail (steeping tea) in a prison setting. Tobias's horrified response to tea instructions plays as either an overreaction or an understandable response to George Sr.'s unsettling domesticity.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I've been in this family for 16 years and I don't think we've ever had a conversation.' / Tobias: 'I'm not gonna discuss this.' / 'You hate me.' / 'Yeah, I do hate you. You took my daughter...'
jokes.setup: George Sr. attempts emotional vulnerability with Tobias
jokes.punchline: Tobias's immediate 'I'm not gonna discuss this' and then his immediate emotional outburst proves he will absolutely discuss this
jokes.explanation: Tobias's instant flip from 'I won't discuss this' to a full emotional flood is standard sitcom irony, but executed competently and true to character.

jokes.text: Tobias therapy-talks George Sr.: 'Fear has turned into hate. So Frightened Inmate #2 isn't frightened at all. He's angry. He's a crabby old coot... and he needs love, just like everyone.' / George Sr.: 'Get your... hands off of me.' / Tobias: 'Shh. Don't be scared.'
jokes.setup: Tobias applies his therapeutic method (previously used on White Power Bill) to George Sr.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s extreme discomfort with being touched and therapized while the 'Frightened Inmate #2' role-research framing is transparently self-serving is the joke; Tobias's 'Don't be scared' to a man who just threatened him is the cap
jokes.explanation: Tobias applying the same therapeutic framework that worked on White Power Bill to George Sr. is a satisfying structural callback. George Sr.'s vulnerability and rage meeting Tobias's unshakeable clinical warmth ('Shh. Don't be scared.') is a great character clash.

jokes.text: Tobias's diagnosis of George Sr.: 'He's a crabby old coot, and he needs love, just like everyone.' Then hugs George Sr. while George Sr. says 'Get your hands off of me.' Tobias: 'Shh. Don't be scared.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has had a therapeutic breakthrough with George Sr.
jokes.punchline: 'He's a crabby old coot' as the clinical diagnosis, followed by an unsolicited hug while George Sr. protests. Tobias's 'Shh. Don't be scared' while George Sr. is clearly furious is a great inversion.
jokes.explanation: 'Crabby old coot' as professional therapeutic language is a good gag. The hug and 'Shh. Don't be scared' being delivered to a furious man who is emphatically not scared is a nice inversion. Tobias's physical warmth against George Sr.'s physical resistance is solid cringe.

jokes.text: Lucille arrives at the magic show: 'We just got here. I couldn't find my keys. I can never find anything in here.' — she has an enormous bag
jokes.setup: Earlier established she was worried about a purse that goes with her outfit
jokes.punchline: She's now carrying an enormous bag and can't find anything in it — a callback to the purse joke, with the visual of an impractical giant bag
jokes.explanation: The callback to the purse joke is satisfying — she's found a bag, and it's useless. Functional character callback with a visual payoff.

jokes.text: Gob at the magic show: 'Where once there was a yacht, now there is naught!'
jokes.setup: Gob dramatically announces the disappearance of the yacht
jokes.punchline: 'Naught' rhymes with 'yacht' in a forced, magician-patter rhyme that is exactly as cheesy as Gob's persona requires
jokes.explanation: 'Naught/yacht' is groan-worthy in exactly the right way for Gob's character — a man who thinks this is sophisticated. The fact that he's just sunk a $900,000 yacht makes the cheesy magician rhyme darkly hilarious in retrospect.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Michael, a magician never reveals his...' / '...I sunk it! I sunk the yacht!'
jokes.setup: Michael asks how Gob did the trick; Gob begins the classic 'magician never reveals' line
jokes.punchline: He immediately breaks and confesses — with enthusiasm — that he literally sank a $700,000 yacht. The reversal happens in a single breath.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's top moments. The setup ('a magician never reveals his...') is a perfect fake-out — we expect the cliché — and Gob's immediate reversal is both a character revelation (he CAN'T keep a secret) and a plot revelation (he literally sank the yacht). The structure is near-perfect: classic setup, complete subversion, maximum information density. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in several dimensions.

jokes.text: 'You sank a $700,000 yacht?' / 'It's $900,000 worth of insurance on it. And got rid of the evidence. How about a "good job"?'
jokes.setup: Michael is horrified by the sinking of the yacht
jokes.punchline: Gob corrects the value upward (insurance perspective), reveals he's committed insurance fraud, destroyed evidence, and then asks for praise — all in rapid succession
jokes.explanation: The 'good job' request is the emotional callback — Gob has committed insurance fraud and destroyed evidence of his father's crimes, and his primary concern is getting validation from his brother. The correction of $700K to $900K of insurance value (recognizing only the profit, not the crime) is brilliant character specificity.

jokes.text: 'You killed Nana!' / 'She's lying! Nana wasn't on that yacht. She's fine. She's been dead for six months.'
jokes.setup: Lucille accuses Gob of killing Nana; Lindsay refutes it
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's defense of Gob — 'She's fine. She's been dead for six months' — is structured as a reassurance that pivots into a worse revelation
jokes.explanation: The line 'She's fine. She's been dead for six months' is structurally perfect and highly quotable. 'She's fine' reads as reassurance; 'She's been dead for six months' is the complete subversion. The logic that being dead means she wasn't on the yacht (so she's 'fine') is both correct and insane. One of the episode's genuinely great lines.

jokes.text: Lucille to banker: 'There must be somewhere I can put this money where it can't be touched.' / 'I suppose you could keep it in your purse.' / 'Very funny, but I'm not carrying a purse.'
jokes.setup: Lucille wants to hide Nana's inheritance money
jokes.punchline: The banker's joke about keeping it in her purse (referencing that she's not carrying one) is a callback to the purse running gag; Lucille's curt 'Very funny, but I'm not carrying a purse' confirms she doesn't find it funny
jokes.explanation: The purse gag has now been seeded three times. The banker's joke lands as a character observation and a callback simultaneously. Lucille's humorless confirmation ('I'm not carrying a purse') is the perfect straight-man response to a joke about herself.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'It's in a trust fund for Annyong.' / 'Annyong.' / 'No one can touch it but me, and I retain control of it until he's 18.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay demands the inheritance money
jokes.punchline: Lucille has put it in a trust for her newly adopted son — ensuring she controls it — and Annyong's 'Annyong' ('hello') response is perfectly timed as either agreement or obliviousness
jokes.explanation: Lucille using her adopted child as a financial vehicle while he cheerfully says 'hello' is darkly funny. The 'Annyong' punctuation at exactly the right moment has become a reliable comic device.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I don't know what's more offensive — the fact you didn't tell us about our grandmother's death or that you sunk a $700,000 yacht!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay confronts Lucille with multiple crimes
jokes.punchline: The moral equivalence between concealing a grandmother's death and sinking a yacht reveals Lindsay's skewed priorities — both are treated as equally offensive
jokes.explanation: Lindsay putting grandmother's death and yacht-sinking in the same moral category is character-revealing. It's also darkly funny that the yacht (money) is what she settles on as equally bad as concealing a death.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Stop lecturing me. I just lost my mother.' — after lying about her mother for months
jokes.setup: Lindsay lectures Lucille about concealing Nana's death
jokes.punchline: Lucille invokes genuine grief ('I just lost my mother') as a defense against being confronted — grief she has apparently suppressed for six months while maintaining a complex lie
jokes.explanation: Lucille deploying genuine grief at maximum strategic convenience — only after being caught — is perfect character comedy. The joke is that she may also genuinely mean it, which is funnier than if she were pure villain.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'Um, well, if he's not here, I'd just have to say he's probably on the yacht.'
jokes.setup: Maeby engineered George Michael going to the yacht; Michael asks where he is; everyone just learned the yacht was sunk
jokes.punchline: Maeby's casual revelation that George Michael is on the yacht — delivered innocently — combined with everyone knowing the yacht just sank creates a beat of pure horror-comedy
jokes.explanation: Perfect convergence of multiple threads. Maeby's casual 'he's probably on the yacht' arrives at exactly the moment when everyone knows the yacht has been sunk. The gap between her casual delivery and the audience's (and other characters') horror is the joke. Structurally, this is one of the best beats in the episode — a sitcom machine firing on all cylinders. Very high impact.

jokes.text: Phone: 'Bluth Company. George Michael speaking, not Kitty.'
jokes.setup: George Michael answers the office phone amid the chaos
jokes.punchline: 'Not Kitty' — he has pre-emptively identified himself to avoid the confusion (and possible phone sex) of being mistaken for Kitty again
jokes.explanation: Two words ('not Kitty') do extraordinary work. George Michael has learned from the 'Talk you off, Pop-Pop?' incident and is now specifying his identity to prevent recurrence. The joke rewards viewers who remember the earlier call. Extremely high economy and quotability.

jokes.text: Michael fires George Michael from the office to give him a real spring break: 'You're fired.' / 'Really? Can you fire people?' / 'Yeah. Go have a spring break.'
jokes.setup: The entire episode has been about Michael being unable to fire Kitty because he 'doesn't have hiring and firing power'
jokes.punchline: Michael fires his own son — which George Michael questions using Kitty's exact logic — and Michael confirms he CAN fire him (even if he couldn't fire Kitty)
jokes.explanation: Beautiful structural callback. George Michael asking 'Can you fire people?' uses Kitty's logic, and Michael's 'Yeah' confirms the absurdity — he can fire his son but not his secretary. The warmth of the moment (Michael giving George Michael spring break) doesn't undermine the comedy; it enriches it.

jokes.text: Michael: 'She'll turn up.' — about missing Kitty — cut to Kitty apparently having been grabbed for evidence
jokes.setup: Michael is confident Kitty will return
jokes.punchline: The episode ends with the implication that Kitty has been disappeared by George Sr.'s fixer — 'she'll turn up' is an ominous unknowing statement
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony of 'She'll turn up' when we have reason to think she won't is effective dark comedy. Works better on rewatch when context is clearer.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Are you sure you're gonna be okay without Kitty?' George Michael: 'Don't you worry. She'll turn up.' [Long pause as Kitty is actually missing]
jokes.setup: Michael is leaving; George Michael reassures him about Kitty
jokes.punchline: 'She'll turn up' — dramatic irony, as Kitty is actually missing (she went looking for the evidence cooler on the yacht that just exploded). The cheerful confidence of this statement against the known facts.
jokes.explanation: Works as both a joke in the moment (overconfident reassurance) and as foreshadowing — 'she'll turn up' takes on darker meaning in the tag scene where she's actively missing and the police are involved. Rewatch bonus for dramatic irony.

jokes.text: On the Next Arrested Development: 'Lupe makes a startling discovery. Oh, so that makes Annyong 18.'
jokes.setup: Earlier, Lucille established the trust fund is hers until Annyong turns 18
jokes.punchline: Lupe's 'discovery' that Annyong is already 18 — instantly negating Lucille's entire financial scheme — is a tag that plants a future plot bomb with minimal setup
jokes.explanation: A tag scene that perfectly dismantles Lucille's plan by revealing Annyong is already 18. The 'Oh, so that makes Annyong 18' delivery is beautifully flat. The information is structured as a consequence that arrives before the audience has even processed the setup as a problem.

jokes.text: 'On the Next AD': Michael is questioned about Kitty's disappearance. 'She showed her knobs in the steakhouse, sir.' — police using 'knobs' as official terminology
jokes.setup: Michael is being questioned about threatening Kitty
jokes.punchline: 'She showed her knobs in the steakhouse, sir' — a police officer using the word 'knobs' in an official context, with total earnestness, as if this is standard police language
jokes.explanation: The word 'knobs' in official police diction is an excellent comic choice — it's British slang deployed earnestly in an American police report. The total deadpan gravity of 'in the steakhouse, sir' makes it funnier. The tag scenes in AD are consistently excellent and this is a good example.

tmdb_overview: Michael attempts to have a one-night stand with an attorney whom he doesn't know is not only blind, but the prosecutor who is trying to put his father in jail. Also, Gob gets married on a dare, Maeby pretends to be a girl with a made up disease, and George Sr.'s admirer turns out to be an undercover SEC agent who has truly fallen for him.
insight: Wedding chaos delivers 56 jokes in 22 minutes, mostly character-driven dark subversion.
